preFix.title: Making a small leather change purse
preFix.subtitle: Cut and saddle-stitch a fold-over coin purse from a single leather panel
preFix.excerpt: How to make a simple fold-over leather coin purse from a single rectangular leather panel, saddle-stitched along two sides and finished with a snap fastener or a button stud to keep the flap closed.
preFix.sourceNotes: Al Stohlman Leathercraft instruction series (public domain editions). Leather Working Guild publications (open access). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A fold-over coin purse is an ideal first leatherwork project: one panel, two seams, and no gussets or zips. The

protects the thread and gives the seam a crisp, recessed appearance. Use 2 to 2.5 mm vegetable-tanned leather, thick enough to hold its shape but thin enough to fold cleanly at the flap crease. Chrome-tanned leather of the same thickness also works and folds more softly.

One leather panel, 180 × 90 mm, 2 to 2.5 mm thick (this gives a finished purse approximately 90 × 60 mm when folded). One snap fastener or button stud. Waxed thread in a matching or contrasting colour. Two blunt-tip harness needles. Stitching groover. Pricking iron or stitching chisel. Edge finish wax or gum tragacanth.
Step 1, cut and mark the fold line

Cut the leather panel to 180 × 90 mm with the leatherwork knife against a metal ruler. Mark the fold line at 90 mm from one short end, this divides the panel into the purse body (90 mm) and the flap (90 mm). Score the fold line lightly on the grain side with the back of the knife blade, just enough to create a fold guide without cutting through; this prevents the leather cracking at the fold in use.
Step 2, bevel, groove, and punch stitch holes

Bevel all four edges of the panel with the edge beveller. Fold the panel at the fold line and align the side edges. Run the stitching groover along both side edges of the folded panel, 3 mm from the edge, on the grain side. Punch the stitch holes through both layers simultaneously with the pricking iron and a mallet, spacing holes at 3 mm intervals, starting 5 mm from the bottom corner and stopping 5 mm from the fold line.

Thread two needles on a length of waxed thread four times the seam length. Saddle-stitch along one side seam: right needle through the first hole, left needle through behind the right thread, both needles pulled snug. Continue to the bottom corner. At the corner, pass the needles through the corner hole twice to lock it. Back-stitch two holes and trim the thread. Repeat on the second side seam.
Step 4, finish edges and fit the closure

Sand the side and bottom edges with the sanding block, working through 120, 180, and 240 grit until the two layers are flush and smooth. Burnish with edge wax. Fit the snap fastener or button stud at the centre of the flap end, using a drive punch for the hole. Apply leather conditioner to the full outer surface.

attrs.fix: Clamp the folded panel with a binder clip at the centre before punching to prevent movement. Punch holes in a single pass through both layers.
attrs.cause: The two layers shifted before or during punching.
attrs.symptom: Stitch holes do not align through both layers when stitching

attrs.fix: Apply a leather conditioner to the fold line and work it in gently before creasing. If the leather is too thick for a clean fold, score the fold line more deeply on the flesh side only (not through to the grain) to allow the fibre to hinge.
attrs.cause: The leather is too thick or too dry to fold without cracking.
attrs.symptom: Leather cracks at the fold crease

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
